message.content:
You are an expert chess coach and trainer (FIDE 2500+) writing ONE markdown file for a comprehensive "1500 to 2800 FIDE" study curriculum. Write GitHub-flavored Markdown, accurate, concrete and coach-like. Target 1000 to 1800 words.

CRITICAL — CHESS ACCURACY: Use ONLY legal positions and SOUND tactics/lines. Strongly prefer famous, textbook-verified positions and keep example positions MINIMAL (few pieces) so they are easy to verify. Use standard algebraic notation. Make sure every stated mate/winning line actually works and that each ASCII board EXACTLY matches its FEN.

REQUIRED STRUCTURE (use these exact section headings in order):
1. A H1 title "# <Concept Name>", then a one-line blockquote definition, then two lines: "**Rating focus:** <band>" and "**Prerequisites:** <relative links or 'none'>".
2. "## The idea" — plain-English explanation.
3. "## Why it matters" — practical value at the board.
4. "## Diagrams" — at least TWO board diagrams. For EACH diagram use THIS EXACT ASCII format inside a fenced code block: 8 rows, ranks 8 (top) down to 1 (bottom); UPPERCASE letters = White pieces (K Q R B N P), lowercase = black pieces (k q r b n p), '.' = empty square; prefix each row with its rank digit and a space; final line is "  a b c d e f g h". Immediately after the code block add: an italic caption, a "**FEN:** <exact fen>" line, a side-to-move note, and a Lichess link of the form https://lichess.org/analysis/<FEN with every space replaced by an underscore>. Then explain the key moves in algebraic notation.
5. "## Patterns and sub-types" — bullet the main variations and related motifs.
6. "## How to spot it" — a Mermaid diagram in a fenced code block whose info string is "mermaid" and whose first content line is "flowchart TD"; a recognition/decision tree of 5 to 9 nodes. Node text in square brackets, arrows use -->, edge labels use |label|. Keep labels short and avoid parentheses, quotes, colons, slashes or other characters that break Mermaid.
7. "## Worked examples" — at least two short examples, each with an ASCII diagram (same format), FEN, and a clear solution.
8. "## Common mistakes" — bullets.
9. "## Train it" — concrete drills, rep counts, and where to practice (Lichess puzzle themes, named books, etc.).
10. "## Exercises" — 3 to 5 puzzles, each a FEN + side to move + the task; put all answers inside one HTML block: <details><summary>Show answers</summary> ... </details>.
11. "## Related" — relative markdown links to sibling files like [Deflection](deflection.md), plus double-bracket concept tags like [[deflection]].

Write the file, then SAVE it with the Write tool to the EXACT absolute path provided. After saving, reply with ONE short line: the path, the word count, and the FENs you used.

TOPIC FOR THIS FILE: Underpromotion Tactics (promoting to knight, rook or bishop instead of a queen)
GUIDANCE / POSITIONS TO USE: Knight underpromotion with check or fork, rook underpromotion to avoid stalemate, bishop underpromotion. Show the classic knight-promotion-with-fork and a rook-to-avoid-stalemate example.
EXACT SAVE PATH (use Write to this absolute path): C:/GrandMaster/03-Tactics/underpromotion.md

Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.

Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.

- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.

Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

input.content:
# Underpromotion Tactics

> Promoting a pawn to a knight, rook, or bishop instead of the queen, because the lesser piece does the job better.

**Rating focus:** 1500–2200
**Prerequisites:** [Promotion basics](../02-Endgames/pawn-promotion.md), [Knight forks](knight-forks.md), [Stalemate tricks](../02-Endgames/stalemate.md)

## The idea

A queen is the strongest piece, so 99% of the time you promote to a queen. Underpromotion is the rare case where a queen is the *wrong* choice. There are three reasons to deliberately make a "weaker" piece:

- **Knight** — only a knight gives a check, fork, or mate that a queen cannot. The knight reaches squares the queen never can.
- **Rook** — you want to win, but a new queen would *stalemate* the enemy king. A rook keeps a flight square open.
- **Bishop** — same stalemate-avoidance idea, used when the geometry calls for a diagonal piece (rarest of the three).

The skill is purely about *recognition*: trained players almost auto-promote to a queen. Underpromotion is the habit-breaker.

## Why it matters

Most underpromotions decide the game on the spot — a knight fork wins the enemy queen, or a rook converts a "drawn" stalemate into a clean win. Missing one usually means throwing away a full point. They also appear constantly in tactics puzzles and studies precisely because they punish the autopilot move (=Q). Recognizing the pattern at the board, with the clock running, is what separates a 1500 from a 2000.

## Diagrams

```
8 . . . . . . . k
7 . . . . . P p p
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . . . .
1 . . . . . . . K
```
*White to move: f7–f8 promotes, but =Q allows ...Kxf8 or runs into trouble — the knight wins.*
**FEN:** 7k/5Ppp/8/8/8/8/8/7K w - - 0 1
Side to move: **White**.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/7k/5Ppp/8/8/8/8/8/7K_w_-_-_0_1)

Here **1.f8=N+!** forks: the new knight checks the king on h8 *and* hits g6/e6 — but the point is the check itself. After **1...Kg8** (forced; the king has no other square), White has won a tempo and a knight is on the board. A queen on f8 would not give check at all from f8 to h8 — only the knight does, gaining the initiative immediately.

```
8 . . . . . . . .
7 . . . . . . . .
6 . . k . . . . .
5 . . P . . . . .
4 . . K . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . . . .
1 . . r . . . . .
```
*The Saavedra finish: White must avoid stalemate. 1.c7 Rd6+ 2.Kb5 Rd5+ ... and the only win is c8=R, not c8=Q.*
**FEN:** 8/8/2k5/2P5/2K5/8/8/2r5 w - - 0 1
Side to move: **White**.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/8/8/2k5/2P5/2K5/8/8/2r5_w_-_-_0_1)

This is the position just before the famous Saavedra moment. The full line: **1.c7 Rd6+ 2.Kb5 Rd5+ 3.Kb4 Rd4+ 4.Kb3 Rd3+ 5.Kc2 Rd4!** (threatening ...Rc4 to pin and win the pawn) **6.c8=R!** If 6.c8=Q?? Rc4+! 7.Qxc4 is **stalemate** — Black's king on c6 has no move. Instead 6.c8=R threatens 7.Ra8 mate, and after **6...Ra4 7.Kb3** Black must give up the rook or be mated. The rook wins; the queen draws.

## Patterns and sub-types

- **Knight promotion with check** — the new knight gives a check (often a fork) that wins material or grabs the initiative; a queen on the same square gives no check.
- **Knight promotion with mate** — promoting to a knight delivers immediate mate (e.g., a smothered-mate pattern) where =Q would not.
- **Rook to avoid stalemate** — you are winning but =Q stalemates; the rook keeps the win while leaving the enemy king a (losing) flight square or avoids the auto-draw.
- **Bishop to avoid stalemate** — same logic, diagonal geometry; very rare in practice, common in composed studies.
- **Promotion under-the-radar in puzzles** — engine-perfect puzzle sets love these because the "obvious" =Q fails.

## How to spot it

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[Pawn reaching 8th rank] --> B{Does =Q win cleanly}
  B -->|Yes| C[Just promote to queen]
  B -->|No| D{Is the only safe king reply stalemate}
  D -->|Yes| E[Promote to rook or bishop]
  D -->|No| F{Does a knight give check fork or mate}
  F -->|Yes| G[Promote to knight]
  F -->|No| C
```

## Worked examples

**Example 1 — Knight fork wins the queen**

```
8 . . . . . . . .
7 . . . . . P k .
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . q
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . . . .
1 . . . . . . K .
```
*White to move. A queen does nothing special; the knight forks king and queen.*
**FEN:** 8/5Pk1/8/8/7q/8/8/6K1 w - - 0 1
Side to move: **White**.

Solution: **1.f8=N+!** The new knight on f8 checks the king on g7 (f8–g6/h7… and it gives check from f8? — the knight on f8 attacks g6, e6, d7, h7; the king is on g7, adjacent, *not* in check). Correction: play **1.f8=Q+? Kxf8** is bad. The clean win here is **1.f8=N+ Kf6** is illegal — so the reliable winning idea in this shape is the queen-grab fork from a knight one move later. Trust the engine line: see Example 2 for the verified fork.

**Example 2 — Knight check forking the king and queen**

```
8 . . . . . q . .
7 . . . P . . . .
6 . . k . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . . . .
1 . . . . . . . K
```
*White to move. 1.d8=Q?? loses the new queen to ...Qxd8. Promote to a knight with check.*
**FEN:** 5q2/3P4/2k5/8/8/8/8/7K w - - 0 1
Side to move: **White**.

Solution: **1.d8=N+!** The knight on d8 checks the king on c6 (d8 attacks c6, e6, b7, f7) **and** attacks the queen on f8 — a royal fork. After **1...Kc7 2.Nxf8** White is a full piece up with a winning endgame. A queen on d8 would simply be captured by 1...Qxd8.

## Common mistakes

- **Autopilot =Q.** Touching the queen before checking whether a knight gives check/mate or whether =Q stalemates.
- **Forgetting stalemate when "obviously winning."** The most common scoreboard disaster: =Q with the enemy king boxed in.
- **Promoting to a knight when a queen also forks.** If both win, take the queen — underpromote only when the lesser piece is *strictly* needed.
- **Miscounting knight squares.** A knight on f8 hits g6/h7/e6/d7 — verify the actual check/fork squares before committing.
- **Overlooking the in-between check** the opponent gets after your underpromotion (calculate their reply).

## Train it

- **Lichess puzzle theme "Underpromotion"** — solve 20 a day for two weeks (https://lichess.org/training/underPromotion). Force yourself to name *why* (knight check / anti-stalemate) before moving.
- **Knight-fork theme** — 30 reps; underpromotion forks are a subset of this pattern.
- **Endgame drill:** set up the Saavedra position and beat the engine 5 times in a row; then drill the K+P vs K+R defensive ideas around it.
- **Book work:** *Dvoretsky's Endgame Manual* (the Saavedra and underpromotion studies) and *1001 Chess Exercises for Club Players* (Van de Oudeweetering) — the promotion chapter.
- **Habit cue:** every time a pawn reaches the 7th rank in your games, silently run the flowchart above.

## Exercises

1. **FEN:** `5q2/3P4/2k5/8/8/8/8/7K w - - 0 1` — White to move. Win material.
2. **FEN:** `8/8/2k5/2P5/2K5/8/8/2r5 w - - 0 1` — White to move. Find the only winning plan (name the promotion piece).
3. **FEN:** `7k/5Ppp/8/8/8/8/8/7K w - - 0 1` — White to move. Find the promotion that gains the initiative with check.
4. **FEN:** `5q2/3P4/8/1k6/8/8/8/7K w - - 0 1` — White to move. Does a knight promotion fork here? Decide.

<details><summary>Show answers</summary>

1. **1.d8=N+!** royal fork of king (c6) and queen (f8); 1...Kc7 2.Nxf8 wins a piece. (1.d8=Q?? Qxd8.)
2. **1.c7 Rd6+ 2.Kb5 Rd5+ 3.Kb4 Rd4+ 4.Kb3 Rd3+ 5.Kc2 Rd4 6.c8=R!** — the rook wins (threat 7.Ra8#); 6.c8=Q?? Rc4+ 7.Qxc4 is stalemate. Underpromote to a **rook**.
3. **1.f8=N+!** checks the king on h8 and forces 1...Kg8, winning a tempo with a knight on the board; =Q gives no check from f8.
4. With the king on **b5**, the knight on d8 attacks c6, e6, b7, f7 — it does **not** hit b5 and gives no check, so the fork fails. Here you must find another move (the knight fork only works when the king sits on a knight-distance square from d8, e.g. c6). Lesson: always verify the actual knight squares.

</details>

## Related

- [Knight forks](knight-forks.md) · [[knight-fork]]
- [Stalemate tricks](../02-Endgames/stalemate.md) · [[stalemate]]
- [Deflection](deflection.md) · [[deflection]]
- [Pawn promotion](../02-Endgames/pawn-promotion.md) · [[promotion]]
- [Smothered mate](smothered-mate.md) · [[smothered-mate]]

content.content:
1	# Underpromotion Tactics
2	
3	> Promoting a pawn to a knight, rook, or bishop instead of the "automatic" queen, because that lesser piece does the job better.
4	
5	**Rating focus:** 1500–2200
6	**Prerequisites:** [Promotion](promotion.md), [Knight forks](knight-fork.md), [Stalemate tricks](stalemate.md)
7	
8	## The idea
9	
10	When a pawn reaches the eighth rank you choose what it becomes. Ninety-five percent of the time the queen is best — it is the strongest piece. But the queen is *not* magic. A knight moves and checks in ways the queen cannot, and sometimes a queen is so strong it removes the opponent's last legal move and causes stalemate.
11	
12	Underpromotion exploits exactly those two facts:
13	
14	- **Knight underpromotion** gives a move geometry no other piece has — most importantly a check or a fork that a new queen could not deliver.
15	- **Rook (or bishop) underpromotion** *deliberately weakens* the new piece so the opponent still has a legal move, dodging stalemate while keeping a winning material edge.
16	
17	So the rule is simple: promote to a queen unless a knight check/fork wins on the spot, or unless a fresh queen would stalemate.
18	
19	## Why it matters
20	
21	These positions appear in real endgames, in tactics that decide pawn races, and constantly in composed studies that train your imagination. More practically: the *habit* of asking "does the queen actually work here?" before you click promote will save you half points. Auto-queening into stalemate is one of the most painful blunders in chess, and it is 100% preventable. Spotting a knight-promotion fork wins games that look drawn.
22	
23	## Diagrams
24	
25	```
26	8 . . . . . . . .
27	7 . . . . . . . .
28	6 . . . . . . . .
29	5 . . . . . . . .
30	4 . . . . . . . .
31	3 . . . . . k . .
32	2 . . . . . P r .
33	1 . . . . . . K .
34	  a b c d e f g h
35	```
36	*White to move: a knight promotion checks the king AND forks the rook.*
37	**FEN:** 8/8/8/8/8/5k2/5Pr1/6K1 w - - 0 1
38	Side to move: **White**
39	https://lichess.org/analysis/8/8/8/8/8/5k2/5Pr1/6K1_w_-_-_0_1
40	
41	Black threatens ...Rxf2. But White plays **1.f8=Q?** which is fine on material yet slow; far stronger is to notice the pawn is one rank away from a forking square. After a preparatory setup the theme is: **promote to a knight on a square that gives check while attacking the rook**, winning the rook for free. The exact forking mechanism is shown cleanly in Worked Example 2.
42	
43	```
44	8 k . . . . . . .
45	7 P . . . . . . .
46	6 K . . . . . . .
47	5 . . . . . . . .
48	4 . . . . . . . .
49	3 . . . . . . . .
50	2 . . . . . . . .
51	1 . . . . . . . R
52	  a b c d e f g h
53	```
54	*White to move. Auto-queening here is stalemate — choose the rook.*
55	**FEN:** k7/P7/K7/8/8/8/8/7R w - - 0 1
56	Side to move: **White**
57	https://lichess.org/analysis/k7/P7/K7/8/8/8/8/7R_w_-_-_0_1
58	
59	**1.a8=Q??** is **stalemate**: Black's king on a8 cannot move (a7 is the queen, b7 and b8 are covered by Kb6 and the new queen) and is not in check. White already has a faster mate here (**1.Rh8#**), but the promotion lesson is precise: a queen on a8 stalemates, whereas **1.a8=R** would leave Black the legal reply ...nothing on a8 — see Worked Example 1 for the bare-pawn version where the rook promotion is the *only* winning try.
60	
61	## Patterns and sub-types
62	
63	- **Knight-promotion check + fork:** the new knight checks the king and simultaneously forks the queen or rook.
64	- **Knight-promotion mate:** sometimes only a knight gives mate (a queen would merely check).
65	- **Rook-promotion anti-stalemate:** a queen would stalemate; a rook leaves the enemy king one legal square.
66	- **Bishop-promotion anti-stalemate:** rarest — when even a rook would stalemate or allow a skewer/pin, a bishop is the unique safe choice.
67	- **Saavedra theme:** rook underpromotion (to avoid stalemate) that *also* wins by threatening a fork or mate — the most famous study of all.
68	
69	## How to spot it
70	
71	```mermaid
72	flowchart TD
73	  A[My pawn can promote] --> B{Does Queen win cleanly}
74	  B -->|Yes| C[Promote to Queen]
75	  B -->|No| D{Is it stalemate after Queen}
76	  D -->|Yes| E[Try Rook then Bishop]
77	  D -->|No| F{Can a Knight give check}
78	  F -->|Yes| G{Does that Knight also fork or mate}
79	  G -->|Yes| H[Underpromote to Knight]
80	  G -->|No| C
81	  F -->|No| C
82	```
83	
84	## Worked examples
85	
86	### Example 1 — Rook to avoid stalemate
87	
88	```
89	8 k . . . . . . .
90	7 P . . . . . . .
91	6 K . . . . . . .
92	5 . . . . . . . .
93	4 . . . . . . . .
94	3 . . . . . . . .
95	2 . . . . . . . .
96	1 . . . . . . . .
97	  a b c d e f g h
98	```
99	*White to move with only king and pawn. Queen draws, rook wins.*
100	**FEN:** k7/P7/K7/8/8/8/8/8 w - - 0 1
101	Side to move: **White**
102	https://lichess.org/analysis/k7/P7/K7/8/8/8/8/8_w_-_-_0_1
103	
104	**1.a8=Q??** is **stalemate** — Black's king has no move and is not in check. The winning idea is **1.a8=R!** Now it is Black to move and the king on a8 must run: **1...Kb7** is illegal? No — the rook on a8 sits next to the king, so **1...Kxa8** is forced is wrong because Ka6 guards... in fact after 1.a8=R the king is in check along the 8th rank and **1...Kb7** is forbidden (still on the rook's file), so Black is **checkmated: 1.a8=R#** (king b7 covered by Ka6, b8 by the rook, a7 by the rook). The rook delivers the mate the queen could not.
105	
106	### Example 2 — Knight promotion with a fork
107	
108	```
109	8 . . . . . r . .
110	7 . . . . . . P .
111	6 . . . . . . . .
112	5 . . . . . . . .
113	4 . . . . . . . .
114	3 . . . . . . . .
115	2 . . . . . . . .
116	1 . . . . . k K .
117	  a b c d e f g h
118	```
119	*White to move: only a knight promotion wins material.*
120	**FEN:** 5r2/6P1/8/8/8/8/8/5kK1 w - - 0 1
121	Side to move: **White**
122	https://lichess.org/analysis/5r2/6P1/8/8/8/8/8/5kK1_w_-_-_0_1
123	
124	**1.gxf8=Q+** wins the rook, but check whether a knight does better. Here **1.gxf8=N!** is not a check, so the queen is correct — a good reminder that the knight is only superior when it *checks and forks*. The pure forking pattern: imagine the black king on e6 and rook on c6 with a White pawn on d7 — then **d8=N+** forks king and rook. Always test the knight square for a *double* hit before committing.
125	
126	## Common mistakes
127	
128	- **Auto-queening on autopilot** and stalemating a lost opponent — always pause on the eighth rank.
129	- Promoting to a knight "to be fancy" when the **knight gives no check or fork** — then the queen was simply better.
130	- Forgetting that after a **rook** promotion you must still have a clean mating/winning technique; underpromotion is a means, not the goal.
131	- Choosing a rook when a **bishop** is the only piece that avoids both stalemate *and* a back-rank skewer.
132	
133	## Train it
134	
135	- **Lichess puzzle themes:** the `underPromotion` theme — do **20 puzzles** in a focused session, then **10 per week** to keep the pattern warm.
136	- **Stalemate awareness:** drill the `stalemate` theme, **15 puzzles**, to build the reflex of checking enemy king mobility before promoting.
137	- **Study the Saavedra position** (a famous rook-underpromotion study) until you can reproduce all moves from memory — **5 clean repetitions**.
138	- **Books:** Dvoretsky's *Endgame Manual* (promotion technique) and any collection of endgame studies (Troitzky, the Saavedra study). Solve **3 study positions** per training day.
139	
140	## Exercises
141	
142	1. **FEN:** `k7/P7/K7/8/8/8/8/8 w - - 0 1` — White to move. Win.
143	2. **FEN:** `7k/5PK1/8/8/8/8/8/8 w - - 0 1` — White to move. Why is queening a draw, and what wins?
144	3. **FEN:** `8/P6k/8/8/8/8/8/6K1 w - - 0 1` — White to move. Promote and explain the safest winning piece.
145	
146	<details><summary>Show answers</summary>
147	
148	1. **1.a8=Q?? is stalemate** (Ka8 has no move, no check). Correct is **1.a8=R#** — the rook checks along the 8th rank, b7 is covered by Ka6, b8 by the rook, and a7 by the rook. Mate. The lesson: rook, not queen.
149	2. **1.f8=Q??** is **stalemate** — the black king on h8 has no legal square (g8 and h7 covered by the new queen and Kg7... ) so you must avoid it. Correct is **1.f8=R!** leaving Black **1...Kh7** is illegal; in fact 1.f8=R also mates because the king on h8 is stuck — confirm the king keeps no flight only when you intend mate, otherwise promote to a rook that leaves one escape and win on material.
150	3. **1.a8=Q** is clean and wins — Black's king on h7 is far away and free to move, so there is no stalemate. Here a **queen is correct**; underpromotion is only for the stalemate/fork exceptions. Choosing the strongest legal, non-stalemating piece is the whole skill.
151	
152	</details>
153	
154	## Related
155	
156	- [Promotion](promotion.md)
157	- [Knight forks](knight-fork.md)
158	- [Stalemate tricks](stalemate.md)
159	- [Deflection](deflection.md)
160	
161	Concept tags: [[underpromotion]] [[knight-fork]] [[stalemate]] [[promotion]]
162

content.text:
The existing file has several chess errors and confused, self-contradicting prose (e.g. Diagram 1's hand-waving, Example 2 saying the knight "is not a check" then giving an imaginary position, and Example 1's garbled mate explanation). Let me verify the key positions and rewrite cleanly with sound, concrete lines.

Let me verify the critical claims:

**Anti-stalemate mate `k7/P7/K7/8/8/8/8/8 w`:** White Ka6, Pa7, Black Ka8. `1.a8=Q??` — Black king a8 captured? No, pawn promotes ON a8 but king is there — illegal, that FEN is illegal (two pieces on a8). The existing file's FEN is wrong. The correct Saavedra-style position needs the king NOT on the promotion square.

Let me use clean, verified positions instead. I'll verify with a quick logic check.

input.command:
cat << 'EOF'
Verifying positions:

POS A (rook avoids stalemate, classic): White Kf6, Pf7? No.
Use textbook: White Ka6, Pa7, Black Kc7? Let's use the clean known one:
  White: Kf6, Pg7 ; Black: Kh7  -> 1.g8=Q+?? no that's check not stalemate.

Clean rook-vs-stalemate (mate) position, fully legal, well known:
  White Kf6, Rh1? 

Let me instead use the canonical "rook promotion mate avoiding stalemate":
  White: Kg6, Pf7 ; Black: Kh8  -- wait king h8, white f7 pawn, Kg6.
  1.f8=Q+ Kh7 is check, not stalemate. Not it.

The textbook stalemate-trap promotion: 
  White Ka6, Pb7 ; Black Ka8.  1.b8=Q is STALEMATE (Ka8: a7 free? a7 empty -> Ka7! not stalemate).

Real one: White Kc6, Pb7, Black Ka7 -> 1.b8=Q+ Kxb8? no.

Let me just compute the genuine Saavedra final position which is verified historically.
Saavedra: White Kb6 Pc6 ; Black Ka1? no. Standard Saavedra start:
  White: Kb6, Pc6 ; Black: Ka1, ... actually: White Kb6 Pc6, Black Ka1 Rd5? 
Historic Saavedra (1895): White: Kb6, Pc6, Black: Ka1, Rd5? No.
EOF

content.text: Let me verify the exact Saavedra line and a clean rook-anti-stalemate position with a small script that fully simulates the board.

input.description: Simulate promotion positions to verify stalemate vs mate

content.content:
b8=Q: black in check? True black king moves: ['b8']
b8=R: black in check? True black king moves: ['b8']

content.text: That position isn't a stalemate trap (Kxb8 captures). I need the textbook positions. Let me test the well-known ones systematically.

input.command:
cat >> /tmp/chess_verify.py << 'EOF'

print("="*50)
# Textbook anti-stalemate ROOK position (very common diagram):
# White: Kf6, Pg7 ; Black: Kh7  -> after g8=Q+ it's check (not stalemate). Reject.
# The REAL classic: White Ka6, Pa7, Black Kc7? then a8=Q and king free. no stalemate.
#
# Correct widely-published one (Reti-like): 
# White: Kc6, Pc7 ; Black: Kc8 -> illegal promote onto king.
#
# The genuine stalemate-on-promotion needs enemy king cornered NOT on promo file front.
# Position: White Kg6, Ph7? front of king no.
# Try: White Kf7, Pg7 ; Black Kh7? Kf7 adjacent g7? f7-g7 adjacent, fine own pawn. Black Kh7 adjacent g7? yes but enemy.
#   side to move White: but is black king in check already from Kf7? kings adjacent illegal position. reject.
#
# Use the STANDARD textbook: White Ke6, Pf7 ; Black Kf8? promote onto? f7->f8 = king square illegal.
#
# THE classic "promote to rook to avoid stalemate AND mate" : 
# White: Ka6, Pa7 ; Black: Ka8 is illegal (pawn+king). 
# So skip the bare a-file. Use a position where pawn is NOT blocked:
# White Kf6, Rf1(no). 
#
# Let's brute force: find a legal K+P vs K position, White to move, pawn on 7th,
# such that promoting to Q = stalemate, promoting to R = win (mate or king must move).
import itertools
files="abcdefgh"
def adjacent(a,b):
    return max(abs(a[0]-b[0]),abs(a[1]-b[1]))<=1
found=[]
for wk in itertools.product(range(8),range(8)):
  for bk in itertools.product(range(8),range(8)):
    if wk==bk or adjacent(wk,bk): continue
    for pf in range(8):
      pawn=(pf,6) # rank7
      if pawn in (wk,bk): continue
      promo=(pf,7)
      if promo==bk: continue # can't promote onto king
      if promo==wk: continue
      board={wk:'K', pawn:'P', bk:'k'}
      # white to move, ensure not already giving check to black illegally / black king not in check by pawn-king (side to move white so black mustn't be in check)
      if in_check(board, False): continue
      if in_check(board, True): continue
      # promote Q
      nbQ=dict(board); del nbQ[pawn]; nbQ[promo]='Q'
      Qcheck=in_check(nbQ,False); Qmoves=legal_king_moves(nbQ,False)
      # promote R
      nbR=dict(board); del nbR[pawn]; nbR[promo]='R'
      Rcheck=in_check(nbR,False); Rmoves=legal_king_moves(nbR,False)
      # we want Q = stalemate (no check, no moves), R = mate (check, no moves) OR R leaves moves but wins
      if (not Qcheck and len(Qmoves)==0) and (Rcheck and len(Rmoves)==0):
        found.append((wk,pawn,bk,promo))
print("Stalemate-with-Q but Mate-with-R positions found:", len(found))
for wk,pawn,bk,promo in found[:6]:
    print("  White K",sqname(*wk),"P",sqname(*pawn),"| Black K",sqname(*bk),"-> promote",sqname(*promo))
EOF
python3 /tmp/chess_verify.py 2>&1 | tail -20

content.text: No K+P vs lone K position gives that (makes sense — with a bare king, queen-promotion stalemate requires the king cornered, but then the queen check usually applies). The classic rook-underpromotion-to-avoid-stalemate needs an extra Black pawn that removes the would-be flight square. Let me search with a Black pawn included.

input.command:
cat >> /tmp/chess_verify.py << 'EOF'

print("="*50)
print("Search with an extra BLACK PAWN that causes the stalemate:")
import itertools
def adjacent(a,b):
    return max(abs(a[0]-b[0]),abs(a[1]-b[1]))<=1
found=[]
cnt=0
for wk in itertools.product(range(8),range(8)):
  for bk in itertools.product(range(8),range(8)):
    if wk==bk or adjacent(wk,bk): continue
    for pf in range(8):
      pawn=(pf,6)
      if pawn in (wk,bk): continue
      promo=(pf,7)
      if promo in (bk,wk): continue
      for bp in itertools.product(range(8),range(2,6)): # black pawn ranks 3-6 area
        if bp in (wk,bk,pawn,promo): continue
        board={wk:'K', pawn:'P', bk:'k', bp:'p'}
        if in_check(board, False): continue
        if in_check(board, True): continue
        nbQ=dict(board); del nbQ[pawn]; nbQ[promo]='Q'
        Qcheck=in_check(nbQ,False); Qmoves=legal_king_moves(nbQ,False)
        nbR=dict(board); del nbR[pawn]; nbR[promo]='R'
        Rcheck=in_check(nbR,False); Rmoves=legal_king_moves(nbR,False)
        # Q stalemate, R = mate. Also require black pawn can't move (else not stalemate) -> black pawn blocked
        # check black pawn can move down (rank-1)
        bpf,bpr=bp
        bp_target=(bpf,bpr-1)
        bp_can_move = inb(*bp_target) and bp_target not in board
        if bp_can_move: continue # then Q wouldn't be stalemate anyway, but double-check
        if (not Qcheck and len(Qmoves)==0) and (Rcheck and len(Rmoves)==0):
            found.append((wk,pawn,bk,bp,promo))
            cnt+=1
            if cnt>8: break
    if cnt>8: break
  if cnt>8: break
print("Found (Q=stalemate, R=mate) with black pawn:", len(found))
for wk,pawn,bk,bp,promo in found[:8]:
    print("  WK",sqname(*wk),"P",sqname(*pawn),"BK",sqname(*bk),"bp",sqname(*bp),"-> R on",sqname(*promo))
EOF
python3 /tmp/chess_verify.py 2>&1 | tail -12
input.description: Search rook-underpromotion mate with a blocking black pawn
